turns.content: I'm reading through this chapter on resource management and the author keeps asking about the difference between a standard Drop implementation and an async version. Can you explain what's actually different between using impl Drop and implementing the AsyncDrop trait?

The core distinction is that regular Drop runs on the main thread when the value goes out of scope, while AsyncDrop requires the drop logic to be async so it can run concurrently with other work. Since AsyncDrop isn't stable yet, most crates don't implement it directly; instead people use patterns like implementing Drop that spawns a task or using third-party libraries like tokio-util's sync-drop to handle the synchronization manually.
That makes sense about the instability. But what if I just want to delay the cleanup until later without waiting for it to finish immediately? Is there a way to simulate that behavior?

Yes, you can defer the drop by wrapping your type in another structure that holds a handle to the cleanup task. For example, you could store a tokio::task::Handle or use a simple Arc<()>) and trigger an async function later via a channel. This avoids blocking the main thread until the resource is actually released.
Wait, so I'm not using AsyncDrop directly then? That seems like a workaround since it's unstable. What exactly am I avoiding?

You're avoiding the need to implement the full AsyncDrop trait which requires careful handling of the executor context. By deferring cleanup, you keep your code compatible with current stable Rust without relying on unstable features or complex async drop logic.
Okay, so I can just use a regular Drop that starts an async task? What happens if the task panics?

If the deferred task panics, it won't propagate immediately because it's running asynchronously. You need to handle errors explicitly in your cleanup logic or use error channels like std::sync::mpsc::Sender so failures don't silently disappear.
Hmm, that sounds risky. What about using a library instead of writing my own wrapper? Are there crates I should know about?

There are libraries like async-drop-sim or tokio-util's sync-drop crate that provide pre-built wrappers for this pattern. They handle the task spawning and error propagation so you don't have to write boilerplate code yourself.
I think I understand now, but what if I need actual async logic inside my Drop? Like sending data over a network before dropping?

In that case you'd implement the defer pattern more explicitly. You could use an Arc<()>) to hold a shared reference and then spawn a tokio::spawn task in your Drop implementation that sends the data asynchronously before letting the type go out of scope.
So I'm basically building my own async drop simulator? That sounds like a lot of work compared to using AsyncDrop directly.

Exactly, that's why the workaround exists. Since AsyncDrop is unstable and tricky to use correctly in production code, most developers prefer the defer pattern with explicit task spawning or third-party crates instead of trying to implement the trait directly.
I see. One last thing though—what happens if I try to drop a type that already has an async cleanup running?

If you're already in the middle of an async cleanup and then drop again, you'll get double-execution issues unless your design ensures idempotency. Usually you'd use a flag or counter to track whether cleanup has started so it only runs once.
Got it. So the key takeaway is that async drop simulation is about managing state carefully when using deferred patterns instead of relying on unstable traits?

Exactly. You manage state by ensuring cleanup only runs once, use stable crates or manual wrappers to avoid AsyncDrop's instability, and accept that you're trading some simplicity for control over when resources are released.

Imagine I'm writing a network client that owns a socket and needs to drop it right before a long-running async operation. Since the current standard for dropping resources asynchronously is unstable, how do I handle this in my code?

You can't rely on an unstable trait yet, so you should implement your own Drop simulation using a custom type that wraps the socket and uses a defer pattern to ensure cleanup happens later. You might create a struct like AsyncSocketGuard that holds a reference or ownership of the socket and implements traits such as DerefMut so it behaves exactly like a normal handle until it's dropped.
That makes sense, but what if I need the resource to be usable during the async phase without blocking? Can I just use a simple wrapper? What about the actual Drop trait implementation details here?

A simple wrapper won't work because the standard Drop trait runs synchronously on thread exit. You must delay that execution until after your async block completes using a defer pattern where you store a closure or callback in an Arc and attach it to a future or channel. This way, when the socket is truly dropped at the end of the program, your deferred cleanup code runs.
So I'm not implementing AsyncDrop myself but deferring the actual Drop? What about thread safety if multiple tasks might need this pattern? How do I manage that in a production environment?

You should use Arc to share ownership of the defer data across threads since it's lock-free and safe for concurrent access. When you have multiple async tasks needing cleanup, each task will hold its own clone of the Arc pointer, and they all point to the same underlying control flow logic that manages the actual Drop timing.
Okay, so I'm managing state with Arc and deferring until drop? What about error handling if something fails during the async phase before cleanup runs? Do I need special traits for this?

You'll want to implement a custom trait similar to std::error::Error but tailored for your deferred logic, ensuring that any errors captured during the async phase can be propagated correctly when the defer finally executes. This allows you to check if an error occurred before actually dropping the resource.
Got it, so I'm building my own synchronization layer? Is there a crate or library already doing this since AsyncDrop is unstable? Or should I write everything from scratch?

There isn't a mature crate yet because AsyncDrop itself is experimental and unstable. You should probably implement the defer pattern yourself using standard crates like tokio for async runtime, but keep in mind that you're building your own solution since no one-off package exists to replace this pattern today.
I see, so I'm writing custom logic? What about testing this? How do I verify my deferred Drop actually runs when the program exits?

You should write unit tests that simulate the full lifecycle by wrapping your custom defer struct in a test harness that forces an immediate drop or uses a mock runtime to trigger the final cleanup. You might also use integration tests where you run the async service and then check if your cleanup function was called exactly once.
Alright, so I'm building my own solution with Arc and custom traits? What about performance implications of this extra layer? Is it noticeable in real-world usage?

The overhead is minimal because you're mostly using standard synchronization primitives like Arc and RefCell which are highly optimized. The main cost comes from the logic itself, not the synchronization, so as long as your defer pattern avoids unnecessary allocations or blocking operations, performance stays close to normal.
Thanks for clarifying. One last thing—what happens if I try to use this in a multi-threaded environment where threads might race on my defer logic? How do I prevent that?

You must ensure your Arc-based structure is shared atomically and that the deferred closure only executes once after all async work completes. By design, since you're deferring until program exit or explicit shutdown, there's no concurrent access during the critical section unless you explicitly synchronize using channels or mutexes before the defer runs.

I'm trying to build a custom async cleanup tool for our CI pipeline, but I keep hitting issues with resource management. It feels like standard RAII patterns don't apply here since the resources need to be released after an async operation completes.

That's a common pain point when moving away from synchronous lifetimes. The core issue is that `Drop` runs synchronously on stack unwinding, which blocks your async runtime if you try to use it for cleanup. You might be tempted to implement `AsyncDrop`, but that trait remains unstable and isn't part of the stable API yet.
I checked the docs and saw a mention of something like `Future` or `Pin` involved in dropping items. Can I just use `std::future::ready` with an async block to simulate this behavior?

You're on the right track thinking about futures, but you can't simply wrap a drop implementation in a future without careful handling of lifetimes and pinning. The `async` keyword creates a `Future`, but dropping the resource inside that future requires moving it into the future's state or using a wrapper type.
So I need to manually implement a custom wrapper? Like a struct that holds the resource and implements some trait?

Exactly. You'll likely create a wrapper struct around your resource, then implement `Drop` and potentially a `Future` trait manually if you want to defer the actual drop until after an async operation finishes.
Wait, but I thought implementing `Future` would make it async? Then why does my cleanup still run before the task completes?

Because if you drop the wrapper too early in your async function, the resource is lost immediately. You need to ensure the drop happens only after the async work finishes, which means deferring it until the end of the scope or using a channel to signal completion.
Can I use `tokio::spawn` to offload the cleanup? That way the main thread can continue while the drop happens?

Spawning a task won't help with the immediate drop behavior you're seeing. The issue is that `Drop` runs before the async block completes unless you explicitly defer it. You might need to use a deferred pattern where you hold a reference and only invoke cleanup after the async operation finishes.
I'm getting confused about whether I should implement `Deref` or just store ownership directly. Does the borrow checker care about this?

The borrow checker cares because you're trying to move a resource into an async context without proper pinning guarantees. If your wrapper doesn't correctly handle `Pin` and `Future`, it will panic at compile time.

Ah, I think I see now. Instead of trying to force-drop before the async block, I should just move the resource into a separate future that runs after my main logic completes. Like wrapping it in `std::future::Future` with a custom `poll` method?

That's your aha moment. By deferring the drop to a separate future that only polls when the async operation is done, you avoid the premature release issue. You can use a simple struct with `poll` implemented manually to ensure the resource stays alive until needed.

I've read about the async drop simulation pattern, but I still don't fully understand how it actually works in a real scenario versus just using standard RAII. I'm stuck on why we can't always rely on the normal Drop trait.

Think of the regular Drop trait like a fire alarm that rings immediately when you leave the room, whereas async drop simulation is more like a delayed notification system where the cleanup task gets queued to run later. Since AsyncDrop is currently unstable and not part of the stable API yet, we often have to implement workarounds using channels or thread-local storage to defer resource release until the main task completes.

That analogy makes sense about timing, but I'm confused on how you actually structure a worker that needs cleanup without blocking the event loop. Can't we just use a simple channel to send the drop signal and then have an executor process it?

Exactly, using a channel is one of the most common workarounds for this problem since AsyncDrop isn't stable. You would create a named pipe or mpsc channel to broadcast when resources are dropped, then run a separate background task that consumes those messages and performs the actual cleanup logic asynchronously.

So if I'm implementing this in my current project with Tokio, how do I actually wire up the defer pattern without creating too many extra threads? I want to avoid spawning a new thread for every single resource drop.

You can achieve this by using a single global executor or a shared task that listens on the channel, rather than spawning per-resource threads. This way you maintain one background worker that handles all the deferred drops efficiently without bloating your thread count.

Okay, so I can use a single thread to handle multiple drop requests via channels, but what about error handling if the cleanup fails? If my background task panics, won't that block the entire application?

Good catch. You should wrap your deferred work in a try_block or use tokio::select with timeout to prevent blocking, and ensure cleanup failures are logged rather than causing panics that halt execution.

I see the logging approach makes sense. But I'm still not sure how I'd integrate this into an existing service that already uses standard Drop for most resources. Is there a way to make it transparent?

You can wrap your existing types in a custom wrapper that implements the necessary traits while delegating actual cleanup to your async handler, making it feel almost transparent to callers who just need to handle resource acquisition and release.
That sounds promising. But what about memory safety? If I'm deferring drops across threads or async contexts, am I risking double frees or use-after-free bugs?

The key is ensuring your defer pattern only schedules a drop once per resource instance. You can track ownership state in the wrapper to guarantee each item gets cleaned up exactly once, preventing those common memory safety issues.
I'm worried about testing this though. How do I verify that my deferred drops actually happened and didn't get lost during shutdown?
You can add counters or a drop registry to your wrapper types that tracks how many items are waiting for cleanup, then assert these values reach zero before the application exits.
That's helpful. One more thing though - what happens if I need to cancel an ongoing async operation mid-stream? Will my deferred drops still execute?

If you're using proper cancellation tokens throughout your system, the defer mechanism can be designed to respect those signals and skip unnecessary cleanup work if the operation is cancelled before completion.

I'm familiar with the standard RAII pattern using lifetimes and the Drop trait to clean up resources when a value goes out of scope, but I've heard that implementing an asynchronous drop is generally considered unsafe in stable Rust. Is there any way to simulate async cleanup without relying on the unstable AsyncDrop feature gate?

You are correct that AsyncDrop is currently behind a feature flag and cannot be used in stable code. The idiomatic workaround for this scenario involves using the std::task::ready() function inside your Drop implementation to defer execution until the next event loop tick, or you can use a separate async task spawned via tokio::spawn to handle cleanup after the main scope is dropped. This effectively simulates async behavior by moving the work into the background thread pool.

I see how std::task::ready() works with the Drop trait, but I'm confused about the safety guarantees since we're deferring execution. If a panic occurs in the cleanup task after the main scope is dropped, will it affect the program's exit code or cause undefined behavior?

The deferred cleanup tasks run on their own thread pool and do not block the current drop process, so panics within those tasks generally won't propagate back to the main scope. However, if you want stronger guarantees you can use a pattern where you implement a custom trait that wraps your resource management logic, ensuring the cleanup is either completed or safely deferred via a channel before the object actually drops.

That sounds promising but I'm wondering about the performance implications of spawning tasks for every single resource. If we have hundreds of resources being managed in a high-frequency trading engine, will this overhead be acceptable?

For most cases using tokio::spawn is efficient enough since modern thread pools handle thousands of concurrent tasks well, but you should only spawn tasks when the actual cleanup logic cannot complete synchronously. For simple resource releases like file handles or connections that can be closed immediately, it's better to use synchronous Drop implementations to avoid unnecessary context switches and scheduler overhead.

I understand the trade-off now, but I'm still curious about how we can detect if a resource actually needs async cleanup before deciding whether to spawn a task. Is there a way to make this decision at compile time rather than runtime?

You cannot easily determine if a specific operation is asynchronous just by looking at the code without running it, since Rust's type system doesn't expose enough information about side effects or timing. However, you can use macros like proc_macro::apply to analyze the cleanup logic and add compile-time checks that warn developers when they might be missing an async requirement.

That macro approach sounds useful but I'm worried it might complicate our codebase significantly. Is there a simpler alternative we could consider for onboarding new developers who need to understand these patterns quickly?

A good alternative is to document clear examples showing when AsyncDrop would be necessary versus when synchronous cleanup suffices, and provide a helper function that wraps the common async pattern so developers can reuse it without worrying about thread safety or panic propagation.

I appreciate the documentation suggestion but I'm still concerned about the complexity of managing multiple threads. What happens if we accidentally drop a resource twice—once in the sync path and once via our deferred task?

Double dropping is indeed problematic and can lead to double-close errors or memory corruption, so you must ensure your wrapper logic checks whether the cleanup has already been performed before attempting another operation. This is typically handled by using a flag or an atomic boolean that tracks whether the resource has been released.
That makes sense but I'm wondering if there's a way to make this pattern more ergonomic so developers don't have to manually track these flags every time they implement cleanup logic?

You can use a generic wrapper struct that automatically manages the state for you by implementing traits like std::ops::DerefMut or custom traits that handle the conditional logic internally, effectively hiding the flag management from the user.

That sounds very ergonomic but I'm still concerned about how this interacts with our existing error handling system. If a cleanup task fails to complete before the program shuts down, will it cause unexpected errors?

If you use tokio's graceful shutdown hooks or implement custom signal handlers that catch panics from spawned tasks, you can ensure clean termination even if async cleanup fails. You might also consider using a channel to report errors back to the main thread before the program exits.

I think I understand the error reporting mechanism now but I'm wondering about the impact on our existing testing framework. Will unit tests that don't run in an event loop still be able to verify these async patterns?

Unit tests without a full event loop will need to use tools like tokio::test or mock implementations of your custom wrapper traits to simulate the async behavior, ensuring they can verify the logic without needing to actually run the application.

That's helpful but I'm still curious about how this interacts with our existing Rust types and crates we already use. Will these patterns work well with libraries like serde or chrono that might need cleanup?

Yes, these async drop patterns integrate seamlessly with standard crates by wrapping their types in your custom RAII structure, allowing them to benefit from the deferred cleanup without modifying the original library's API.
I'm building a network client and I want to use RAII patterns, but I keep running into issues where dropping resources blocks the async runtime. Is there a way to make drop fully asynchronous?

You're thinking about AsyncDrop which is currently unstable in the nightly compiler, so it's not reliable for production yet. The stable approach is to defer cleanup until the task completes using the Defer trait from the async-drop crate instead of trying to implement an unsafe drop function.
That sounds better than what I was doing, but can't I just wrap my struct in a Box and let the compiler handle it? I think if I make the fields async then dropping will be fine too?

Wrapping in a Box won't solve this because Box::new still executes drop synchronously even for async types. Making fields async doesn't automatically delay the final cleanup of the entire struct. The real solution is using Defer to schedule work that runs after the task ends without blocking the executor.
So I should create a wrapper type that stores a Defer instance? And what exactly does Defer do under the hood?

Yes, you create a wrapper struct that holds an Arc<RefCell<Defer>>. The Defer trait lets you add code to run after the async block finishes, so your cleanup runs in a separate task instead of during drop.
Can I just use std::thread::spawn for this? Threads are simpler than whatever Defer does?

Using threads isn't ideal because it breaks the async abstraction and can cause deadlocks in some scenarios. Defer is designed to work within the tokio runtime without spawning extra threads, keeping your architecture clean.
Okay so I avoid std::thread and just use Defer? Does this mean my code will never block again?
Not entirely, but you eliminate the blocking drop issue. Your cleanup runs in a dedicated task that completes after your main function returns, so the executor isn't held up.
I'm thinking of async drop simulation like a deferred cleanup function in Python, but I want to know how Rust handles this since the standard library doesn't have an AsyncDrop trait yet.

Right, so there isn't a built-in AsyncDrop trait because the async drop mechanism is still experimental and unstable. The common workaround is to manually defer cleanup by storing a closure in a field that gets executed later or using a custom Drop implementation with std::thread::spawn for background tasks.
That sounds risky though, since spawning threads might cause race conditions. Is there a safer pattern to avoid explicit thread management?

You're right about the risks. A safer approach is to use a deferred drop pattern where you store a function pointer or closure in a struct that implements Drop, but instead of running it immediately, you schedule it on an executor like tokio::spawn for async work.
So I'd use tokio::spawn to run the cleanup later? But what if the program exits before that task completes?
That's a good catch. If you're using tokio::spawn, the task will only complete when the runtime is shut down, so it won't run if the process exits early unless you handle shutdown logic explicitly.
What about using std::sync::Mutex to protect shared state during async operations?

Using std::sync::Mutex is fine for synchronous access, but if you're doing actual async work with tokio, consider using std::sync::Arc combined with a mutex or use the newer std::sync::Mutex if you need non-atomic operations.
I'm confused about whether I should implement Drop manually or rely on automatic cleanup. When exactly do I trigger the async drop?

You should manually implement a custom Drop trait for your struct if you need to defer cleanup, and use tokio::task::spawn_local or similar patterns to ensure the async work happens at the right time.
Can I combine this with std::sync::Arc to share state across threads safely?
Yes, you can wrap your struct in std::sync::Arc and pass it around, but remember that the async drop logic still needs careful handling to avoid deadlocks or race conditions.
What about using a state machine pattern instead of manual Drop implementations?
A state machine can help manage the lifecycle, but for async drops specifically, you'll still need to implement custom logic since there's no built-in support yet.
So the key takeaway is that I have to manually handle cleanup and avoid relying on automatic Drop unless it's fully synchronous?
Exactly, for async scenarios you'll need to implement custom cleanup logic using patterns like deferred drop with tokio::spawn or similar executor-based approaches.

I'm trying to understand how to handle cleanup in async code, and it feels a lot like the std::mem::drop function I've seen everywhere else. But when I try to use AsyncDrop directly, it seems unstable. Is there a way to simulate dropping resources without breaking my current design?

You're right that AsyncDrop is currently in the process of stabilization and isn't fully ready for production yet. The most common pattern right now involves using the defer! macro from crates like async-drop or implementing a custom Drop trait wrapper manually. This allows you to schedule cleanup tasks that run after the future completes without requiring full async support on all drop targets.
That sounds helpful, but I'm worried about memory safety if I don't get the timing wrong. Can I just use a regular Drop implementation and wrap it in an await block?

You cannot simply wrap a synchronous Drop inside an await block because that would prevent the compiler from running the drop logic at the right moment. Instead, you should create a new type that implements Drop and schedules its cleanup work using the defer! macro or by manually calling tasks::spawn to offload the heavy lifting until the end of the scope.
Ah, so I shouldn't be trying to await inside a normal Drop. But what if my cleanup logic itself needs async operations? How do I structure that?

When your cleanup requires async work, you need to defer the actual execution until after the current scope has finished its synchronous part. You can achieve this by wrapping your type in a closure or using a struct that holds a reference to the async task and ensures it runs only when the parent scope drops.
I think I'm starting to see where things get tricky. If I use defer! macro, does it automatically handle the cancellation if the future is aborted?

The defer! macro from crates like async-drop will attempt to run your cleanup even if the parent task was cancelled, though you should be aware that some resources might not fully clean up if there's an early abort. For critical data loss scenarios, manual tracking with a flag or using tokio::task::JoinSet can give you more control.
I'm getting confused about whether I should use JoinSet or just stick with the macro approach. Is one better for my specific case?

If you need fine-grained control over which tasks are spawned and how they interact, JoinSet is more flexible. However, if your cleanup pattern is simple and consistent across many components, the macro approach from crates like async-drop is usually sufficient and less verbose.
Okay, so for a single resource with complex cleanup logic, should I implement my own Drop trait wrapper?

Yes, implementing a custom Drop trait wrapper is often the cleanest solution when you need to manage multiple async tasks. You can define a struct that holds your resources and a task handle, then implement Drop to ensure all pending tasks are properly joined before dropping.
Wait, I thought the macro approach was simpler. Why would I go through the extra step of implementing my own wrapper?

The macro is great for simple cases, but when your cleanup involves multiple independent async tasks or complex state management, a manual Drop implementation gives you explicit control over the lifecycle and error handling. It's worth the extra boilerplate to avoid subtle race conditions.
Got it, so I should implement a wrapper that spawns all my async tasks and then joins them in Drop. That makes sense now.

Exactly. By spawning each cleanup task inside your wrapper's constructor and joining them in the Drop implementation, you ensure everything runs to completion before the outer scope leaves. This pattern avoids the instability of AsyncDrop while maintaining safety.
Oh! So I don't need to worry about the unstable AsyncDrop trait anymore if I use this defer pattern instead?

Yes, that's the key insight. By deferring async work until after synchronous scopes complete and using explicit task joins in a Drop implementation, you sidestep the need for unstable traits entirely while achieving the same safety guarantees.
Imagine I'm building a network client in Rust that needs to drop TCP sockets while the main task is still running, and I want to test how my code handles this without blocking.

In Rust you can't reliably use AsyncDrop directly because it's unstable; instead you should wrap your socket in a type that implements Drop with deferred logic or use the std::task::Context to schedule cleanup. You might also consider using tokio::sync::mpsc channels to communicate shutdown signals.
I thought I could just implement the AsyncDrop trait directly on my socket wrapper and let it run in the background thread, that would be simpler than managing separate channels.

That won't work because AsyncDrop is currently unstable and doesn't guarantee safety; your code might panic or leak resources if used incorrectly. The trait requires specific runtime support that isn't fully stabilized yet, so relying on it breaks compilation in most current toolchains.
Okay then I'll use a regular Drop implementation with async blocks inside, but how do I ensure the cleanup actually runs without needing to spawn threads?

You can't run arbitrary async code directly in Drop; instead you should implement the std::task::Poll::Ready pattern or use a deferred drop pattern where you store a closure that gets executed later. The proper approach is to avoid immediate async execution and instead schedule cleanup via futures.
So I need to separate the drop logic from the actual resource deallocation, maybe by using a channel or a state machine?

Exactly; you can use channels like tokio::sync::mpsc to signal when cleanup should happen, or implement a finite-state machine that transitions from active to closed before dropping. This avoids needing direct async execution inside Drop and keeps your code predictable.
I'm still confused why the compiler complains about my AsyncDrop attempt even though I added #[async] manually; what exactly is wrong with that approach?

The compiler errors because there's no stable implementation of an async-capable Drop trait yet; your manual #[async] attribute doesn't satisfy the language's safety guarantees. The Rust standard library hasn't finalized a way to run cleanup tasks asynchronously within drop semantics.
Got it, so I should just avoid using AsyncDrop entirely and use regular Drop with some external coordination?

Yes; stick to stable traits like std::ops::DerefMut or custom wrappers that implement Drop without async. Use a deferred pattern where you store a closure in a separate future that runs cleanup only when appropriate, not inside the destructor itself.
Can I use the tokio::spawn macro to trigger this cleanup later instead of relying on Drop?

You can spawn a task with tokio::spawn, but be careful not to lose ownership; ensure the spawned closure captures exactly what it needs and doesn't panic. This gives you explicit control over when cleanup runs without needing unstable async drop features.
What about using a custom RAII wrapper that implements Drop but defers actual socket close until the task ends?
That's a good pattern; you can create a wrapper struct that holds a handle to your socket and delays closing it. When the main task completes, that wrapper is dropped and closes the socket cleanly.
So the key insight is using stable Drop semantics with external coordination instead of unstable async drop features?
Precisely; you avoid relying on AsyncDrop by combining regular Drop with channels or task-based deferral. This keeps your code compatible and safe while still achieving asynchronous cleanup behavior.
What is the difference between a regular drop and an async drop simulation in Rust?

In standard Rust, you use Drop to ensure cleanup happens immediately when ownership transfers or goes out of scope. However, for non-blocking I/O like networking, the Drop trait can block waiting on a connection close event. An async drop simulation mimics this behavior by deferring cleanup until after a synchronous operation completes, often using channels or threads instead of blocking the main thread.
I'm familiar with RAII patterns in Java where objects are dropped automatically. How does Rust handle this differently for async scenarios?

Rust uses the Drop trait to manage cleanup, but unlike Java's garbage collector which might delay reclamation, Rust guarantees immediate execution unless you intentionally defer it. For async scenarios, you can't rely on Drop alone because it blocks; instead, you implement a defer pattern where cleanup is scheduled for later or handled in a separate thread.
Can you explain the defer pattern I should use when writing this?

A common approach is to wrap your resource in a struct that holds a channel or uses an executor to schedule cleanup. You start with a regular Drop implementation, then refactor it so the cleanup logic runs asynchronously via an internal thread pool or by sending messages through a channel for later processing.
Is there a crate that provides this out of the box?

The tokio crate is commonly used for async runtime support, but it doesn't provide an AsyncDrop trait directly since AsyncDrop itself is unstable. You might look at crates like tokio-util or implement your own wrapper around the executor to manage cleanup timing.
What about using the Drop trait with a channel for signaling?

Yes, you can use a channel to signal when cleanup should occur. The resource holds a sender end of the channel, and when dropped, it sends a message indicating that cleanup is ready. A separate task listens on this channel and performs the actual cleanup without blocking the main thread.
Does this avoid the issues with unstable AsyncDrop?

Yes, by avoiding the use of AsyncDrop entirely. Instead, you manually manage the async lifecycle using stable traits and channels, ensuring your code remains compatible without relying on experimental features.
What about using an executor to handle this?
You can use the tokio::runtime::Runtime to spawn tasks that handle cleanup. This allows you to defer the work until after synchronous operations complete, ensuring no blocking occurs.
I understand now. Let me think about how this would look in my code.
Great. Imagine wrapping your resource in a struct that sends a message to an executor when dropped, then has a separate task that listens for the signal and performs cleanup asynchronously.
That makes sense. It's like having a background worker that picks up tasks only after they're done with the current batch.
Exactly, it's similar to how Java uses background threads for cleanup, but Rust does this more explicitly and safely without needing explicit thread management or blocking operations.
So the key is separating ownership from actual execution timing?
Yes, that's it. You decouple when the resource is released (ownership) from when cleanup actually happens (execution), giving you flexibility and performance.
